title: Rwandan Genocide begins
summary: On 7 April 1994, hours after a surface-to-air missile brought down the plane carrying Rwandan President Juvenal Habyarimana over Kigali the previous evening, an organised campaign of mass killing began that would, over roughly one hundred days, take the lives of an estimated 500,000 to 800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu Rwandans. Directed by a Hutu Power clique inside the state and executed by soldiers, the Interahamwe militia, and mobilised civilians, the genocide was one of the most concentrated exterminations of the twentieth century. The world's failure to stop it became a defining scandal of the post-Cold War order.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The categories Hutu and Tutsi long predated the genocide, but their hardening into rigid ethnic identities owed much to European colonial rule. Precolonial Rwanda had been a centralised kingdom in which Tutsi, Hutu, and Twa were social and occupational as much as ethnic categories, with intermarriage and mobility between them. German and, from 1916, Belgian administrators, following the racial anthropology of the day, treated Tutsi cattle-keepers as a superior 'Hamitic' aristocracy of supposed Ethiopian origin, and Hutu cultivators as subordinate 'Bantu'. In 1933 the Belgian authorities issued identity cards recording each Rwandan's ethnicity, an administrative act that would prove lethal sixty years later; the cards remained a formal requirement into the 1990s.

The 1959 Hutu 'social revolution', sanctioned by Belgium as decolonisation approached, overturned the Tutsi monarchy and drove tens of thousands of Tutsi into exile in Uganda, Burundi, and Zaire. At independence on 1 July 1962 Rwanda became a Hutu-dominated republic under President Gregoire Kayibanda. Successive pogroms in 1963, 1973, and later years produced new waves of Tutsi refugees; by 1990 the diaspora numbered close to 500,000. Rwanda itself was one of the most densely populated countries in Africa, its economy dependent on coffee and tea exports whose world prices collapsed in the late 1980s, deepening land pressure and unemployment.

President Juvenal Habyarimana, an army general who took power in a 1973 coup, ruled a one-party state through the Mouvement Republicain National pour la Democratie et le Developpement (MRND), drawing his inner circle, the akazu ('little house'), from his northern home region and from his wife Agathe's family. In October 1990 the Rwandan Patriotic Front (RPF), a mainly Tutsi guerrilla army formed from the Ugandan diaspora and commanded by Paul Kagame, a former Ugandan military intelligence officer, invaded northern Rwanda. The ensuing civil war intensified Hutu-Tutsi polarisation and gave Habyarimana's regime a pretext for repression. Under international pressure, the Arusha Accords were signed on 4 August 1993, envisaging a power-sharing transitional government, integration of the two armies, and the return of refugees. A United Nations peacekeeping mission, UNAMIR, was dispatched that October to oversee implementation under Canadian Lieutenant-General Romeo Dallaire, initially with 2,548 personnel.

Within the ruling MRND, however, a Hutu Power faction rejected the Accords. Its ideologues broadcast anti-Tutsi propaganda through the newspaper Kangura, which in December 1990 had published the 'Hutu Ten Commandments' proscribing intermarriage and business with Tutsis, and, from July 1993, through Radio Television Libre des Mille Collines (RTLM), which mixed popular music with dehumanising rhetoric that called Tutsis 'inyenzi' (cockroaches). Machetes were imported by the hundreds of thousands from China; lists of prominent Tutsis and moderate Hutus were drawn up commune by commune; the Interahamwe ('those who attack together'), the youth wing of the MRND, was trained as a militia by army officers. In January 1994 Dallaire cabled UN headquarters warning that an informant, code-named Jean-Pierre, had disclosed plans to exterminate the Tutsi population and had shown him hidden weapons caches; he requested authority to seize the weapons. Headquarters, under future secretary-general Kofi Annan, instructed him not to act. The machinery of extermination was already in place; it awaited only a signal.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
At approximately 20:25 on 6 April 1994 a Dassault Falcon 50 jet carrying President Habyarimana of Rwanda and President Cyprien Ntaryamira of Burundi, returning from a summit in Dar es Salaam, was struck by two surface-to-air missiles on its approach to Kigali airport. All aboard were killed. Responsibility for the assassination has never been conclusively established; French and Rwandan investigations have variously blamed Hutu extremists opposed to the Arusha Accords and the RPF, and the question remains politically live.

Within hours a crisis committee dominated by Colonel Theoneste Bagosora, chef de cabinet of the defence ministry, took effective control of the country. Roadblocks manned by soldiers and Interahamwe militia sprang up across Kigali. Killing began that night. Among the first victims were the moderate Hutu Prime Minister Agathe Uwilingiyimana, hacked to death on the morning of 7 April, and the ten Belgian UNAMIR soldiers who had been guarding her, who were tortured and murdered at Camp Kigali. The killing of the Belgians was a calculated act: within days Brussels had withdrawn its contingent, and the UN Security Council on 21 April voted to reduce UNAMIR from 2,548 personnel to just 270.

Over the following weeks the killings spread from the capital to almost every commune in the country. Bourgmestres and prefects mobilised the population; Hutu civilians were compelled, often on pain of death, to join in the murder of their Tutsi neighbours. Churches, hospitals, and schools, where terrified Tutsis had gathered in the hope of sanctuary, became the sites of the largest single massacres: at Nyarubuye Catholic Church on 15 April, at the Ntarama and Nyamata churches in Bugesera, at the Murambi Technical School on 21 April, where an estimated 45,000 people were killed in a single day. RTLM read out the names and hiding places of those to be killed. Systematic rape of Tutsi women, estimated in the hundreds of thousands, accompanied the murders.

The RPF meanwhile resumed its offensive from the north. Its 14,000 disciplined soldiers advanced on Kigali against a disintegrating Rwandan army, halting massacres in the areas they captured but unable to stop the killing elsewhere. On 22 June, with the RPF closing on the capital, France launched Operation Turquoise, ostensibly a humanitarian mission but which effectively provided a safe corridor for retreating genocidaires and their families into Zaire. The RPF took Kigali on 4 July and declared a ceasefire on 18 July. The genocide had lasted roughly a hundred days.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Mass graves at the Nyamata Genocide Memorial in Rwanda, one of the sites where thousands of Tutsi civilians were killed in April 1994.

sections.body:
The scale of what had been done was, and remains, difficult to grasp. Estimates of the dead range from about 500,000 to over 800,000, with a widely cited figure of some 800,000; the government of Rwanda has since put the number at just over one million. The great majority were Tutsi; Hutu who opposed the killing or were suspected of doing so were also murdered in large numbers. The demographic destruction of a small country, with a pre-genocide population of around eight million, was almost total in some communes.

As the RPF advanced, an estimated two million Hutu, ordinary civilians mixed with genocidaires, fled westward into eastern Zaire, where cholera killed tens of thousands in refugee camps around Goma in July 1994. The camps became bases for the former regime's army and militia, whose cross-border raids destabilised the Great Lakes region for a decade. In 1996-1997 Rwanda and Uganda backed a Zairean rebellion led by Laurent-Desire Kabila that overthrew Mobutu Sese Seko, renaming the country the Democratic Republic of the Congo. The subsequent Congo Wars, of 1996-1997 and 1998-2003, drew in nine African armies and cost, by conservative estimates, several million lives.

Judicial reckoning was extensive but incomplete. The UN Security Council established the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR) in Arusha, Tanzania, on 8 November 1994. Over more than two decades it indicted 93 people, convicting 62, including former prime minister Jean Kambanda, who pleaded guilty in 1998 in the first such plea by a former head of government, and Colonel Bagosora, sentenced to life imprisonment in 2008. Inside Rwanda, some two million cases were processed through community-based gacaca courts between 2005 and 2012, delivering imperfect but rapid accountability.

Internationally, the genocide became a byword for failure. President Bill Clinton, on a visit to Kigali in March 1998, described the failure to act as 'the deepest regret of my presidency'. UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan, who as head of peacekeeping had received Dallaire's warning cables, later commissioned an independent inquiry that concluded the UN had failed the Rwandan people. The Belgian and French governments issued qualified apologies; France's role, in particular, remained bitterly contested until a 2021 French historical commission report acknowledged 'serious and overwhelming responsibilities' short of complicity.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Modern Rwanda is a country reconstructed under the shadow of April 1994. Paul Kagame, the RPF commander who effectively ended the genocide, became vice-president and defence minister in July 1994 and president in 2000. His government has abolished ethnic categories on identity documents, criminalised 'divisionism' and genocide denial, invested heavily in health, education, and information technology, and built a reputation for administrative discipline, low corruption, and one of Africa's fastest growth rates. Kigali is often held up as one of the continent's best-run cities; life expectancy has more than doubled since 1994. Critics inside and outside Rwanda argue that this stability rests on tight political control, restrictions on independent media, and treatment of dissidents, both at home and abroad, that has drawn sustained international criticism, including allegations of extraterritorial assassinations.

The genocide reshaped international law and doctrine. It contributed to the establishment of the International Criminal Court in 2002, and to the emergence of the 'Responsibility to Protect' (R2P) doctrine, endorsed at the 2005 UN World Summit, which holds that state sovereignty carries a duty to prevent mass atrocity. That doctrine has been unevenly applied since, invoked in Libya in 2011, contested over Syria, Darfur, Myanmar, and Xinjiang, but the memory of Rwanda continues to weigh on debates over intervention. The ICTR's jurisprudence, especially the 1998 Akayesu judgment, established rape as a constitutive act of genocide for the first time.

Memorial sites at Nyamata, Ntarama, Murambi, and Gisozi, where more than 250,000 victims are buried, preserve the physical evidence, bones, clothing, weapons, of what took place, and receive tens of thousands of visitors a year. 7 April is observed annually as Kwibuka, the national day of remembrance, opening a hundred-day mourning period concluding on Liberation Day, 4 July. Rwandan and international scholars, novelists, and filmmakers, from Philip Gourevitch's We Wish to Inform You That Tomorrow We Will Be Killed with Our Families (1998) to the film Hotel Rwanda (2004) and Jean Hatzfeld's oral histories, have shaped global memory of the event.

For historians, the Rwandan genocide is unusual for the speed and mass participation of the killing: neighbours murdering neighbours, largely with edged weapons, at a rate that per-day exceeded that of the Nazi extermination camps. Understanding how a modern African state, in the space of weeks, converted its own citizens into instruments of extermination remains one of the great problems of contemporary history, and a warning about the interaction of ideology, radio, political panic, and long-manufactured hatred.

worldContext.text: In April 1994 the Yugoslav wars were entering their fourth year. The siege of Sarajevo continued; NATO had carried out its first-ever combat operation on 28 February, shooting down four Serb aircraft over Bosnia. The European Union had come into being on 1 November 1993 with the entry into force of the Maastricht Treaty. In Italy, the Christian Democrat-dominated postwar political order had collapsed under the Tangentopoli corruption investigations, and Silvio Berlusconi's Forza Italia won the general election of 27-28 March 1994.

The United States under President Bill Clinton was preoccupied with domestic priorities including healthcare reform and NAFTA, which had taken effect on 1 January 1994. On the same day the Zapatista uprising began in Chiapas, Mexico. In Haiti, a US-led intervention in September 1994 would restore the democratically elected president Jean-Bertrand Aristide. Cuba was in the depths of the 'Special Period' economic crisis that followed the loss of Soviet subsidies. Colombia's cartel wars continued; Pablo Escobar had been killed in December 1993.

The Oslo peace process was in its optimistic first phase. The Declaration of Principles had been signed on the White House lawn in September 1993; the Cairo Agreement of 4 May 1994 established Palestinian self-rule in Gaza and Jericho. On 25 February 1994 an Israeli settler, Baruch Goldstein, had murdered 29 Palestinian worshippers at the Ibrahimi Mosque in Hebron. Algeria's civil war between the government and Islamist insurgents continued to intensify; South Yemeni forces attempted secession in May, triggering a brief civil war.

North Korea's Great Leader Kim Il-sung died on 8 July 1994, ten weeks after the Rwandan genocide began; his son Kim Jong-il succeeded him. A nuclear crisis over Pyongyang's programme culminated in the Agreed Framework signed with the United States in October. China under Jiang Zemin was continuing rapid market reform in the wake of Deng Xiaoping's 1992 southern tour. Japan's political system was unstable: the coalition government of Morihiro Hosokawa, the first non-Liberal Democratic Party government in 38 years, collapsed in April 1994.

India under Prime Minister P. V. Narasimha Rao was three years into economic liberalisation. The Bombay bombings of March 1993, retaliation for the demolition of the Babri Masjid in December 1992, had left communal tensions raw. In Afghanistan, mujahideen factions who had overthrown the communist government in 1992 fought one another for control of Kabul; a religious militia calling itself the Taliban was forming that year around the town of Kandahar. Sri Lanka's civil war with the LTTE continued.

figures.name: Juvenal Habyarimana
figures.role: President of Rwanda 1973-1994; assassinated in a plane shootdown on 6 April 1994

figures.name: Theoneste Bagosora
figures.role: Colonel and chef de cabinet of the Rwandan defence ministry; principal organiser of the genocide, later convicted by the ICTR

figures.name: Paul Kagame
figures.role: Commander of the Rwandan Patriotic Front; halted the genocide, later president of Rwanda
figures.credit: Village de la Francophonie, 2024, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Romeo Dallaire
figures.role: Canadian lieutenant-general commanding UNAMIR; his warnings before the genocide were ignored

figures.name: Agathe Uwilingiyimana
figures.role: Moderate Hutu Prime Minister of Rwanda; murdered on 7 April 1994

images.caption: Mass graves at the Nyamata Genocide Memorial in Rwanda, one of the sites where thousands of Tutsi civilians were killed after taking refuge in a Catholic church in April 1994.

sources.title: Outreach Programme on the 1994 Genocide Against the Tutsi in Rwanda
Shake Hands with the Devil: The Failure of Humanity in Rwanda
